nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with sandy or gravel substrate and moderate to strong current; found in the Mekong, Chao Phraya, Xe Bangfai, and Meklong river basins. Often associated with submerged rocks, root tangles, and macrophyte patches in mid-water.

decorPreferences: multiple caves and rocky overhangs for territory definition
dense planting around tank perimeter (Amazon swords, vallisneria, Java fern)
open sandy or fine-gravel midground for foraging
line-of-sight breaks to reduce inter-specimen aggression if multiple fish are attempted
powerhead or spray bar to simulate moderate current

diet.notes: Benthic omnivore and opportunistic algae grazer. Accepts high-quality sinking pellets, algae wafers, spirulina-based flake, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach). Also takes frozen and live foods: bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, tubifex. In the wild feeds on algae, organic detritus, small invertebrates, and plant matter scraped from substrate surfaces. Varied diet with regular vegetable component supports health and reduces fin-nipping tendency.

goodWith: mid-water schooling fish (large tetras, barbs, danios)
corydoras catfish (with caution — avoid bottom overlap in small tanks)
loaches in large tanks with ample territory
medium-to-large peaceful cichlids in spacious tanks
avoidWith: other Rainbow Sharks (solitary in tanks under 400 L — intense conspecific aggression at maturity)
Red-Tailed Black Shark (Epalzeorhynchos bicolor, fw-fish-124) — closely related, triggers severe territorial conflict
Flying Fox (Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus) — same genus, aggressive interspecific competition
Bala Sharks and other similar-looking Cyprinids in restricted space
small slow-moving or long-finned fish that share the bottom zone
dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates (predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo repository for Epalzeorhynchos frenatum. Check current availability; FishBase photo pages require direct access to confirm license status.
Wikimedia Commons category for E. frenatum; multiple images from aquarium specimens typically available under CC BY-SA. Confirm specific image license before use.

summary: A boldly coloured Southeast Asian cyprinid with a dark grey-to-black body and vivid red-orange fins — not a true shark (Cyprinidae, not Carcharhinidae). Grows to 15 cm and becomes strongly territorial at maturity; keep singly in most home aquaria. Distinguished from the Red-Tailed Black Shark (E. bicolor) by red coloration on all fins, not just the caudal fin.
careNotes: The Rainbow Shark (Epalzeorhynchos frenatum) is a popular, active bottom-dweller from the river systems of mainland Southeast Asia — primarily the Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Xe Bangfai basins in Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam. Despite the common name it is a true cyprinid, unrelated to sharks; the name refers to the shark-like body profile and erect dorsal fin. Adults reach 12–15 cm and are characterized by a dark charcoal-grey body with uniformly red-to-orange fins (dorsal, pectoral, pelvic, anal, and caudal), distinguishing them from the closely related Red-Tailed Black Shark (E. bicolor), which has a fully black body and a red caudal fin only. An albino selectively-bred form (pale/white body with red fins and pink eyes) is widely available in the hobby under the trade name 'Albino Rainbow Shark'. A minimum tank of 200 L with a footprint of at least 120 cm is recommended for a single adult. Provide abundant hiding places — caves, overturned terracotta pots, rocky overhangs, and driftwood — as territorial ownership of a defined area reduces stress and aggression towards tankmates. Moderate current via a powerhead or spray bar mimics native riverine conditions and is appreciated. Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 5–15), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5), and warm (22–27°C). The species is strongly territorial towards conspecifics and closely related genus members at sexual maturity (typically 12–18 months). In tanks under approximately 400 L, only a single specimen should be kept. Groups in very large (400 L+) heavily decorated tanks with multiple distinct territories have been reported by advanced keepers, but this is not recommended for beginners. Avoid housing with E. bicolor (Red-Tailed Black Shark, fw-fish-124) and Epalzeorhynchos kalopterus (Flying Fox) under any circumstances — all are genus-mates and will engage in escalating territorial disputes that typically result in injury or death. Mid-water schooling fish such as barbs, danios, larger tetras, and rainbowfish make suitable tankmates as they occupy different water columns. The Rainbow Shark is an active grazer on algae films and organic detritus and will help maintain substrate cleanliness; it supplements this with invertebrate prey and should be offered a varied diet including vegetable matter.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria; commercial breeding is achieved at scale in Southeast Asia using hormone injection (HCG or carp pituitary extract). In captivity the species is an egg-scatterer but the extreme territorial aggression at maturity makes pairing extremely difficult without very large, purpose-built facilities. No successful captive spawning accounts have been widely documented in hobbyist literature without hormonal intervention. Sexing is difficult — mature females may appear slightly deeper-bodied. Not a viable breeding project for hobbyists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Epalzeorhynchos frenatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 15 cm), temperature range (22–26°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (2–15 dH), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, Xe Bangfai, Meklong basins), biotope (river), aggression profile (strongly territorial, solitary recommended), diet (benthic omnivore), and breeding notes (hormone-induced in commercial farms). Tank size recommendation: minimum 200 L for single specimen.

additional.name: FishBase — Epalzeorhynchos frenatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), adult size (to 15 cm TL), native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins, Thailand/Laos/Cambodia/Vietnam), temperature (22–27°C), and diet classification (omnivore). Confirmed species is not found in Sumatra or Borneo (those records belong to E. kalopterus).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rainbow Shark Care
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Confirmed tank size (55 gal / ~208 L minimum), temperature (72–79°F / 22–26°C), pH 6.5–7.5, semi-aggressive temperament, solitary keeping recommendation, and distinction from Red-Tailed Black Shark. Noted albino variant availability and compatibility considerations with mid-water species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rainbow Shark Profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist reference. Confirmed adult size (up to 15 cm), temperament (territorial, aggressive towards conspecifics and related species), care level (intermediate), diet (omnivore with algae grazing), tank decoration advice (caves, dense planting, line-of-sight breaks), and genus-mate conflict with E. bicolor and E. kalopterus.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Epalzeorhynchos bicolor (for comparative species separation)
additional.notes: Reference entry for the Red-Tailed Black Shark (E. bicolor, fw-fish-124 in DB) used to accurately document morphological distinction: E. bicolor has a fully black body with a red caudal fin only; E. frenatum has a dark grey body with red on all fins. Also confirmed mutual aggression between the two species should they be housed together.

fish.breedingNotes: Commercial production relies on hormone injection (HCG/carp pituitary extract) in Southeast Asian fish farms. Hobbyist spawning is not reliably documented; extreme territorial aggression at maturity makes pairing in home aquaria impractical.
